title: The Four Winds
subtitle: The main types of market risk

hook.metaphor: A sailor learns to name the winds, because each behaves differently and demands a different response. Market risk has its own prevailing winds — four main ones — and knowing which is blowing tells you what to watch and how to brace. Confuse them and you trim the wrong sail at the wrong moment.
hook.scenario: A globally-active company worries about 'market risk' in general — but its real exposures are very specific: it borrows at floating rates, sells abroad in three currencies, holds a share portfolio, and depends on copper. 'Market risk' is too vague to act on. How should it break the problem down?

learn: **Interest-rate risk** — the wind from the price of money. When rates move, borrowing costs and the value of bonds and loans shift. It hits borrowers, lenders, and anyone holding fixed-income assets (rising rates push existing bond prices *down*).

**Currency / FX risk** — the wind from exchange rates. Anyone earning, paying, or holding value in a foreign currency is exposed: a favourable export contract can be wiped out by an adverse currency move before you're even paid.
**Equity risk** — the wind from share prices. Holding shares (directly, or via a pension or investment fund) means your value rises and falls with the stock market's mood.

**Commodity risk** — the wind from raw-material prices: oil, gas, metals, wheat. Airlines, manufacturers, farmers, and food companies live and die by these. Our copper-dependent company is squarely exposed here.

The practical point: 'market risk' is too vague to manage — you must **decompose** it into specific exposures (this currency, that rate, this commodity) before you can measure or hedge anything. Our globally-active firm doesn't have 'market risk'; it has floating-rate risk *plus* three FX exposures *plus* equity *plus* copper. Naming each wind is the first step to handling it.
bridge: So at work, you can turn a vague worry about 'the markets' into something actionable by naming the winds: *which specific exposures do we have — interest rate, FX, equity, commodity — and how big is each?* You can't hedge or measure 'market risk' in the abstract; you manage named, sized exposures one wind at a time.

interactions.title: Which wind is blowing?
interactions.prompt: Sort each exposure into the type of market risk it represents.

items.label: A pension fund holding lots of shares
A bond portfolio that loses value as rates rise
An exporter paid in foreign currency
A bakery exposed to wheat prices
A company on a floating-rate loan
A refiner exposed to the price of crude oil

interactions.title: Too vague to act
interactions.prompt: The firm worries about 'market risk' generally. What's the right first move?

options.label: Buy one product to cover 'market risk' overall.
options.feedback: There's no single instrument for 'market risk' in the abstract — its exposures are distinct (rates, three currencies, equities, copper). You can't hedge a vague worry.

options.label: Decompose it into specific, sized exposures, then tackle each.
options.feedback: Exactly. Name the winds: floating-rate risk, each FX exposure, equity, copper. Only once each is identified and measured can you decide what to manage and how.

options.label: Ignore it — it's too complicated to break down.
options.feedback: Decomposing is precisely what makes it manageable. 'Too complicated' usually means 'not yet broken into named exposures'.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Market risk blows as four main winds — interest rate, FX, equity, and commodity — and since you can't manage 'the markets' in the abstract, you name and size each specific exposure first.”

checkpoint.q: The four main types of market risk are…
checkpoint.explain: Each is a distinct 'wind' from a different kind of price.

checkpoint.q: When interest rates rise, the price of existing fixed-rate bonds generally…
checkpoint.explain: Older bonds paying lower fixed rates become less attractive, so their market price falls — interest-rate risk.

checkpoint.q: Why decompose 'market risk' into specific exposures?
options: Because you can only measure and hedge named, sized exposures, not a vague whole
checkpoint.explain: Naming each wind (this rate, that currency, this commodity) is the first step to managing it.
